title: Lesson 333
transcript: We just discussed the cryptanalytic attacks, which are all focused on one thing, which is trying to deduce the key. We're now going to talk through the cryptographic attacks. And the cryptographic attacks have one thing in common, and that is they have nothing in common. There's a whole smorgasbord of different stuff. Some of the cryptographic attacks about finding the key, many are not. So in this next series of videos here, we're going to walk through a whole bunch of different types of cryptographic attacks. And we're going to start with a very commonly used, very generic type of attack known as a man in the middle attack. And by the way, we should really, as an industry, be calling this like a person in the middle attack. It seems very genderist to call it a man in the middle attack. But anyways, that's what you'll probably see on the exam. So what is a man-in-the-middle attack? The man-in-the-middle attack, as I mentioned, is a very generic type of an attack where an attacker puts themselves in between two individuals, two users, two systems that are talking to each other. And the attacker intercepts their communications. And this can be for all sorts of reasons. Maybe the attacker just wants to read the information and then pass it on. Maybe the attacker wants to modify the information. Maybe the attacker wants to try and block some of the information. There's all sorts of reasons why a man in the middle attack can be used. Very generic, the attacker puts themselves in the middle of communication between two users, allowing the attacker to intercept and potentially modify those communications. Now, a type of man in the middle attack is known as a replay attack. And the basic idea behind a replay attack is that the attacker records some cryptographic information, can't actually understand it, but then just replays it later on, usually to gain unauthorized access. Let me give you an example of what this would look like. Let's say I'm building a very secure application, and I want my users obviously to log in with a username and a password, and they have to do so across the network. The insecure way of doing this would just be to have my users send their username and password in plain text. If anyone was performing a man-in-the-middle attack, they could easily see my user's credentials, and that would be bad. So to make my system more secure, here's the tricky thing I'm gonna do. I'm gonna get my users to, before they send in their password, I'm gonna get them to first hash their password. I'll get them to leave their username in plain text. That doesn't really matter. But I'll get them to send their password as a hash value across the network. And this is pretty smart, right? Because this way, even... You know, if an attacker is performing a man-in-the-middle attack and captures the hash value, remember, hashing is one way. Just, you know, if an attacker has a hash value, that's not going to allow the attacker to figure out the user's password because hashing is one way. And so this seems brilliant, right? I could get my users to log in by providing their username in plain text and their password as a hash value, send that across the network, and here's what that might look like, right? Our user Alice here is sending the hash value across the network to our server, Bob. But the attacker is capturing a copy of that hash value. And this is exactly what a replay attack is. The attacker is capturing a copy of the hash value. Now, the attacker can't actually figure out the user's password. but the attacker doesn't need to. The attacker doesn't need to know what the user's password is. All the attacker needs to do is later on say like, hey, I'm Alice, and here's the hash value of my password. And the server's going to assume, oh, you know, you provided the username Alice, and you provided the correct hash value, which means you must know the user's password, and therefore I'm going to authenticate you. The baddie here doesn't actually know the user's password. The baddie is performing a replay attack. The baddie is recording some cryptographic information, can't actually understand it, but then just replays it at a later date or at a later time, usually to gain unauthorized access. That's a replay attack. Put one more time, the attacker records some cryptographing information, can't actually decipher it, but then just replays it later on. That's a replay attack, which is a type of man-in-the-middle attack. The final one we're going to talk about here is a pass the hash attack, which is a type of replay attack. And it's actually what I just described there. A pass the hash attack is exactly what I've described where the attacker captures a hash value, can't figure out the user's password, but just replays the hash value later on to gain unauthorized access. So just remember this. Man in the Middle is a very generic type of an attack. A replay attack is a type of Man in the Middle attack. And a pass the hash attack is a type of replay attack.
